items.q: On a bar chart showing the number of pets, the bar for 'cat' has height 7. What does the 7 mean?
items.correct: Seven people (or items) in the survey chose 'cat' — frequency is the count.
items.misconception: Seven cats are owned by the people in total.
items.random_wrong: Cats are the seventh most popular pet.
items.why: Students confuse the value on the frequency axis with the category itself or with a ranking, instead of reading it as a count of responses.

items.q: Which average is best to use for the data set [2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 100]?
items.correct: The median (3.5) — it isn't distorted by the outlier 100, unlike the mean.
items.misconception: The mean — it always gives the most accurate average.
items.random_wrong: The mode, because the most common value is always the best representative.
items.why: Students treat 'the mean' as the default for every data set, without considering whether outliers make a different measure of centre more appropriate.

items.misconception: 15, because 15% means 15 units out of any amount.
items.why: Students treat 'X percent of' as if X is the answer directly, not as a proportion to apply to the amount.

items.q: Total angle of all sectors in a pie chart?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 360°. “180°” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is a slice of a pie chart called?
items.why: It's Sector. “Segment” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 60 people total. A sector of 60° represents how many people?
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
